signals.title: USD — Inflation Shock + Bear Steepener
signals.text: US CPI at 3.8% (April) exceeds forecasts, driven by a 40% contribution from energy costs. The 2s10s yield curve shifts into a Bear Steepener regime (+51.3bp) as US 10Y yields climb to 4.463%. This inflation surprise reignites Fed hike fears, supporting the USD despite neutral spec positioning (long% 35.8%). DXY at 98.30 remains bid as the market rotates into industrials and out of high-growth assets. Implication: USD strength persists until energy-led inflation expectations cool.

signals.title: AUD/JPY — G8 Bilateral Theme + Carry + Retail Divergence
signals.text: AUD/JPY at 114.152 reflects a dominant G8 bilateral theme with a 1.184% spread between the week's leader and laggard. The pair is supported by a +360bp carry advantage (RBA 4.35% vs BoJ 0.75%) and a hawkish RBA bias. Retail sentiment is 87% short, diverging from COT LF net long +58,994 (78.3% long). This institutional vs retail divergence at extreme levels suggests a potential retail capitulation squeeze if price holds above 114.00. Implication: Carry-seeking flows dominate JPY weakness.

signals.title: NZD/USD — COT vs OIS Divergence + RBNZ
signals.text: NZD/USD COT LF net short -19,294 (long% 18.5%) shows speculators heavily fading the currency despite a hawkish RBNZ market bias (↑ Hike). With the RBNZ quarterly Survey of Inflation Expectations due next, any upside surprise creates an acute short-squeeze risk for crowded spec shorts. NZD/USD at 0.5963 faces a +150bp carry deficit vs USD, but the RBNZ meeting on 27 May remains the primary catalyst for a positioning unwind. Implication: Extreme spec shorting leaves NZD vulnerable to hawkish data.

signals.title: USD/JPY — Correlation Break + Carry
signals.text: USD/JPY at 157.649 shows a significant correlation break vs US 10Y yields (corr=+0.525 vs norm=+0.089, z=-1.96σ). While the +300bp Fed-BoJ carry spread keeps the pair bid, the decoupling from Treasury moves suggests JPY is currently more sensitive to domestic BoJ hike expectations (bias ↑ Hike) than US rate volatility. VIX at 18.0 (normal volatility) keeps carry trades viable, but intervention risk remains a factor as price tests recent highs. Implication: Carry remains the primary driver until VIX exceeds 25.
